heading: [Q1 2026 shatters venture funding records as AI absorbs $242bn](https://news.crunchbase.com/venture/record-breaking-funding-ai-global-q1-2026/)
body: Investors allocated $300bn across 6,000 startups globally during Q1 2026, representing growth exceeding 150% compared to both the prior quarter and the same period last year. AI companies received $242bn – 80% of total global venture capital. The concentration is striking: four of every five venture dollars now flow to AI, compressing funding availability for non-AI startups and raising questions about whether valuations in the sector have detached from revenue fundamentals. For biopharma, medtech and insurance companies raising capital, the implication is that AI positioning in pitch materials has shifted from differentiator to table stakes.

heading: [SoftBank deploys first $10bn tranche into OpenAI](https://www.hedgeco.net/news/04/2026/softbanks-10-billion-openai-infusion-the-new-arms-race-for-ai-infrastructure.html)
body: SoftBank Group has deployed the first $10bn of its OpenAI commitment, a move that underscores the convergence between venture capital, sovereign-scale capital pools and AI infrastructure buildout. The investment cements SoftBank's position as OpenAI's largest external backer and gives it significant influence over the company's infrastructure roadmap – including data centre partnerships and chip procurement. For competing foundation model companies, the deal raises the capital threshold required to remain competitive in training runs and inference infrastructure.

heading: [Anthropic's Claude Code source code leaked](https://go.theregister.com/feed/www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/)
body: Anthropic accidentally exposed the source code of Claude Code, its AI coding tool. [Analysis of the leak](https://www.storyboard18.com/amp/digital/claude-code-source-leak-how-anthropics-ai-architecture-exposure-impacts-security-and-rivals-93881.htm) revealed the extent of system-level data collection the tool performs on users' machines, raising privacy concerns and providing competitors with detailed architectural intelligence. The leak offers a rare view into the telemetry design choices of a major foundation model company. For enterprise customers evaluating coding assistants, the episode places data governance and vendor transparency at the centre of procurement decisions – precisely the kind of compliance question regulators are beginning to formalise.

heading: [Chinese chipmakers surge to 41% of domestic AI accelerator market](https://telecom.economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/devices/chinese-chipmakers-surge-to-41-of-ai-accelerator-market-as-nvidias-dominance-declines/129966271)
body: Total shipments of AI accelerator cards in China reached approximately 4m units in 2025, according to IDC, with [domestic manufacturers delivering 1.65m GPUs](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/nvidia-market-share-in-china-falls-to-less-than-60-percent-chinese-chip-makers-deliver-1-65-million-ai-gpus-as-the-government-pushes-data-centers-to-use-domestic-chips) and pushing Nvidia's market share below 60%. Beijing's push for government agencies and major enterprises to adopt domestic alternatives has accelerated following successive waves of US export controls. The structural consequence extends beyond China: a bifurcating chip supply chain means that multinational companies training or deploying models across geographies face divergent hardware stacks, with implications for model portability and inference cost.

heading: [Oracle to cut 30,000 jobs while accelerating AI investment](https://incrypted.com/en/oracle-to-slash-30-000-jobs-while-ramping/)
body: Oracle has initiated a large-scale layoff programme affecting roughly 30,000 employees, with approximately 10,000 already let go across divisions. The company is reallocating resources to cloud services and AI infrastructure – specifically data centre buildout. The move follows Oracle's positioning as an AI infrastructure provider and signals that the workforce composition required for a cloud-and-AI business differs materially from that of a legacy database enterprise. For Oracle's customers in pharma, insurance and manufacturing, the question is whether service quality holds during the transition.

heading: [Google's TurboQuant clears a path to cheaper AI inference](https://go.theregister.com/feed/www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/googles_turboquant_reality/)
body: Google unveiled TurboQuant, an AI data compression technology that reduces memory requirements for model inference. The technique addresses a cost bottleneck – DRAM accounts for a growing share of inference expenses – but the underlying DRAM pricing environment remains tight. For companies running inference workloads at scale, TurboQuant offers a way to extract more throughput from existing hardware, buying time while memory supply catches up with demand.

heading: [Alibaba deploys agentic AI as merchant workforce at scale](https://techwireasia.com/2026/04/alibaba-agentic-ai-ecommerce-digital-workforce-taobao-tmall/)
body:
Alibaba rolled out autonomous digital workers to millions of Taobao and Tmall merchants, betting that the token economy – where AI agents execute transactions on behalf of merchants – is where commercial AI value lands. The deployment is among the largest production-scale agentic AI implementations reported, providing Alibaba with a data flywheel: each merchant interaction generates training data that refines the agents' commercial judgement.
## Biopharma
The regulatory overhang on biopharma AI tightened this week when the FDA's own internal AI tooling became the subject of compliance scrutiny, even as the largest AI drug discovery deal in history was signed. The tension between regulatory caution and commercial ambition defines the sector's current dynamics.

heading: [Eli Lilly and Insilico Medicine sign $2.75bn AI drug discovery alliance](https://www.cnbc.com/video/2026/03/30/hong-kongs-insilico-reaches-2pt75-billion-eli-lilly-deal-for-ai-developed-drugs.html)
body: Eli Lilly announced a global R&D collaboration with Hong Kong-headquartered Insilico Medicine [valued at $2.75bn](https://techstory.in/insilico-medicine-lands-massive-2-75b-global-rd-collaboration-with-eli-lilly/), making it the largest disclosed AI drug discovery partnership. The deal expands Lilly's generative chemistry capabilities, with Insilico contributing its AI platform for target identification and compound design. Insilico also presented [four posters at AACR](https://bioengineer.org/insilico-at-aacr-showcasing-four--posters-highlighting-the-potential-of-generative-ai/) demonstrating its generative AI's application in oncology. The deal structure – weighted toward milestones rather than upfront payments – reveals that Lilly is buying optionality on Insilico's platform rather than paying a premium for validated assets. Competitors without comparable AI partnerships now face a widening capability gap in early-stage drug design.

heading: [FDA scrutinises AI model migration over compliance risks](https://www.aibucket.io/post/fda-scrutinizes-ai-model-migration)
body: Elsa, the FDA's internal AI tool used to assist in reviewing clinical trial documents and regulatory submissions, is undergoing rapid model migration after federal directives restricted the use of Anthropic's Claude. The forced switch raises compliance questions: validation records tied to the original model may not transfer cleanly, and any change in model behaviour during an active review cycle introduces regulatory uncertainty. For sponsors with submissions in the FDA pipeline, the episode is a reminder that their regulator's own AI stack is now a variable in review timelines.

heading: [Mantis Biotech builds 'digital twins' of humans for clinical data generation](https://tech.yahoo.com/ai/meta-ai/articles/mantis-biotech-making-digital-twins-143000600.html)
body: Mantis Biotech is training large language models on vast genomics and clinical datasets to create digital twins of human biology, addressing one of drug development's persistent constraints: data scarcity. The approach generates synthetic patient data that can supplement or replace traditional datasets in early-stage research. If validated, the method would reduce the cost and timeline of trial design. The competitive risk is that companies without comparable synthetic-data capabilities face longer development cycles for the same class of targets.

heading: [MMV and deepmirror launch free AI drug discovery platform for neglected diseases](https://techinformed.com/mmv-deepmirror-launch-free-ai-drug-discovery-platform/)
body: Medicines for Malaria Venture and deepmirror launched Drug Design for Global Health (dd4gh), a free AI drug discovery platform available to eligible researchers working on malaria, tuberculosis and neglected tropical diseases. The platform lowers the barrier to AI-assisted compound design for researchers who lack the budgets to license commercial platforms. The initiative also creates a data-sharing mechanism that could accelerate open-science drug discovery in areas where commercial incentives are weak.

heading: [Pharma Mar partners with Globant on multi-agent AI for cancer drug discovery](https://simplywall.st/stocks/es/pharmaceuticals-biotech/bme-phm/pharma-mar-shares/news/pharma-mar-bmephm-valuation-check-after-new-globant-ai-partn)
body: Spanish pharmaceutical company Pharma Mar partnered with Globant to deploy a multi-agent AI platform for cancer drug discovery, aiming for faster and more accurate lead identification. The partnership pairs Pharma Mar's marine-organism compound library with Globant's AI engineering. For European pharma companies, the deal illustrates a growing pattern: rather than building AI capabilities in-house, mid-cap companies are licensing platform access from specialist providers, accepting the trade-off of shared infrastructure for faster deployment.

heading: [Recursion expands partnership with Citeline for AI-enabled clinical trial platform](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/31/3265558/0/en/Recursion-Expands-Strategic-Partnership-with-Citeline-to-Advance-Its-AI-Enabled-ClinTech-Platform.html)
body:
Recursion extended its strategic partnership with Citeline, integrating Citeline's clinical trial intelligence data into Recursion's AI-enabled ClinTech platform. The integration gives Recursion's models access to richer trial metadata – site performance, enrolment dynamics and competitive intelligence – which feeds the platform's ability to optimise trial design. Each new data integration compounds Recursion's platform advantage, making it harder for competitors to replicate its trial-optimisation capabilities without equivalent data partnerships.
## MedTech and digital health
Regulators are moving in on AI in medtech from two directions: the FDA cleared the first ECG-AI algorithm for pulmonary hypertension detection, signalling what it will approve, while Canadian privacy officials flagged AI scribes as a clinical risk, signalling what it may restrict.

heading: [Anumana receives FDA clearance for first ECG-AI pulmonary hypertension algorithm](https://trial.medpath.com/news/2017aa34e244de22/anumana-receives-fda-clearance-for-first-ecg-ai-algorithm-to-detect-early-pulmonary-hypertension?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
body: Anumana secured FDA 510(k) clearance for the first AI algorithm designed to detect pulmonary hypertension from standard 12-lead ECGs. The algorithm demonstrated 73% sensitivity and 74.4% specificity. The clearance is significant because pulmonary hypertension is typically diagnosed late, and detection via a routine ECG – a device already present in virtually every clinical setting – removes a major equipment barrier to screening. For competitors developing cardiac AI algorithms, Anumana now holds first-mover advantage in this indication and a regulatory predicate that subsequent applicants will need to match or exceed.

heading: [Mount Sinai integrates OpenEvidence AI into Epic EHR enterprise-wide](https://hitconsultant.net/2026/04/01/mount-sinai-openevidence-ai-epic-integration-nurses-pharmacists/)
body: Mount Sinai Health System deployed the OpenEvidence AI platform directly into its Epic electronic health record system, extending evidence-based clinical decision support beyond physicians to nurses and pharmacists. The integration is notable for its scope – enterprise-wide, across professional roles – and for the distribution mechanism. By embedding AI inside the existing EHR workflow rather than requiring clinicians to switch applications, Mount Sinai reduces adoption friction. For OpenEvidence, the deployment creates a reference case that lowers the sales burden at other Epic-based health systems.

heading: [AI scribes in Canada flagged for privacy and safety risks](https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2026/04/ai-scribes-health-care-canada-privacy-safety-risks/)
body: Canada's Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner raised concerns about ambient AI scribes in healthcare, citing risks related to privacy, accuracy and potential bias. A [separate multisite study](https://www.tun.com/home/ai-scribes-ease-doctors-paperwork-modestly-cut-ehr-time/) found that AI scribes trim doctors' EHR time and slightly increase patient visits, but the real-world gains are modest – and an [internal audit at one regional insurer](https://www.swept.ai/post/ai-claims-processing-insurance-automation) found hallucination rates that required retraining. The Canadian privacy intervention signals that regulators in other jurisdictions may impose disclosure or consent requirements on ambient listening tools, adding compliance cost to what vendors have marketed as a frictionless deployment.

heading: [Jimini Health raises $17m for AI mental health chatbot within clinical teams](https://www.statnews.com/2026/03/31/jimini-health-raises-funding-ai-chatbot-sage-mental-health/)
body: Jimini Health secured $17m in seed funding to launch Sage, an AI chatbot designed to support mental health treatment within clinical teams rather than replace human therapists. The startup has raised $25m total. The positioning is deliberate: by integrating into clinical workflows rather than offering direct-to-consumer therapy, Jimini avoids the regulatory and liability exposure that has dogged consumer mental health chatbots. The clinical-team model also creates a natural pathway to payer reimbursement, since the tool supports rather than substitutes a billable provider interaction.

heading: [Avo raises $10m Series A for clinical AI platform](https://www.einpresswire.com/article/900692099/avo-raises-10-million-series-a-to-be-the-clinical-ai-platform-powered-by-trusted-knowledge)
body: Avo raised $10m in Series A funding to build a clinical AI platform powered by curated medical knowledge rather than general-purpose language models. The approach addresses the trust deficit that persists in clinical AI: by constraining outputs to verified medical content, Avo reduces hallucination risk. The $10m raise is modest by AI standards, but the clinical-knowledge curation moat – built through partnerships with medical institutions – is difficult and expensive to replicate.

heading: [Good Samaritan Hospital deploys RapidAI's Lumina 3D imaging](https://www.itnonline.com/content/good-samaritan-hospital-implements-ai-driven-3d-imaging-technology)
body:
HCA Healthcare's Good Samaritan Hospital became the first hospital in the Bay Area to implement Lumina 3D by RapidAI, an AI-driven imaging solution that creates three-dimensional views of blood vessels in the brain. The deployment extends RapidAI's installed base in neurovascular imaging, a category where first-mover installations build clinical evidence that feeds regulatory submissions and purchasing decisions at other sites.
## Complex manufacturing
Supply-chain constraints and pricing pressure defined the manufacturing sector this week, with the regulators' indirect influence felt through export controls that are reshaping chip geography and through capacity bottlenecks that no amount of capital can immediately solve.

heading: [AI boom strains TSMC capacity, opens window for Samsung](https://aninews.in/news/business/ai-boom-strains-taiwans-smc-capacity-opens-door-for-samsung-in-chip-race20260331160909/)
body: Surging AI demand is straining TSMC's production capacity, particularly in advanced packaging – the process of integrating multiple chip components into a single package. [Research from Epoch AI](https://epoch.ai/data-insights/ai-chip-supply-chain-constraints) confirmed that advanced packaging and high-bandwidth memory, not logic die fabrication, were the binding constraints on AI chip production in 2025. The bottleneck creates a structural opening for Samsung, which has invested in its own advanced packaging capabilities. For AI companies planning training runs or large-scale inference deployments, the constraint means longer lead times and higher costs – a physical ceiling on the speed of AI scaling.

heading: [Intel confirms CPU price hikes of 10–15% amid AI-driven supply crunch](https://winbuzzer.com/2026/03/31/intel-confirms-cpu-price-rises-amid-supply-constraints-xcxwbn/)
body: Intel raised CPU prices for OEMs by 10–15% as AI data centre demand stretches lead times from weeks to months. Both Intel and AMD face shortages across server, PC and Chromebook product lines, with some orders now taking up to six months to fulfil. The price increases flow downstream to every company purchasing compute infrastructure – including hospitals, insurance carriers and manufacturing firms deploying on-premises AI.

heading: [Agile Robots completes acquisition of thyssenkrupp Automation Engineering](https://roboticsandautomationnews.com/2026/04/01/agile-robots-closes-acquisition-of-thyssenkrupp-automation-engineering/100337/)
body: Munich-based Agile Robots completed its acquisition of thyssenkrupp Automation Engineering's European and North American assets, a deal initially announced in November 2025. The acquisition gives Agile Robots an installed base of industrial automation customers and engineering capacity that would take years to build organically. For thyssenkrupp, the divestment continues a pattern of shedding non-core units. For European manufacturers evaluating AI-powered robotic integration, Agile Robots now combines software intelligence with hardware deployment capability – a full-stack offering that pure-software competitors cannot match.

heading: [Cognichip raises $60m to apply AI to chip design](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/01/cognichip-wants-ai-to-design-the-chips-that-power-ai-and-just-raised-60m-to-try/)
body: Cognichip raised $60m to build a deep learning model that works alongside engineers during chip design, targeting a bottleneck the industry has endured for decades: design cycles that stretch 18 months or longer. The company claims its model can compress portions of the design flow, reducing iteration time. If validated, the approach would shorten time-to-market for custom AI accelerators – the segment where Broadcom, Marvell and smaller custom silicon firms compete.

heading: [TerraPower and SoftServe deploy AI digital twin for nuclear reactor on Nvidia Omniverse](https://letsdatascience.com/news/terrapower-deploys-ai-platform-for-natrium-80ccdf97)
body: TerraPower and SoftServe unveiled an AI-powered digital-twin platform built on Nvidia Omniverse for the Natrium advanced nuclear reactor, reducing early-stage engineering design cycle times. Nuclear reactor design is among the most heavily regulated engineering disciplines; the ability to simulate and iterate in a digital twin before committing to physical prototyping lowers both cost and regulatory risk. The deployment also serves as a reference case for Nvidia's Omniverse platform in regulated heavy industry.

heading: [Memory shortage deepens as AI demand creates supply-demand imbalance](https://www.digitimes.com/news/a20260331PD216/2026-demand-data-price-competition-ddr5.html)
body:
Global memory chip shortages have shifted industry focus from price competition to securing supply. Advanced production capacity is being prioritised for AI memory products – particularly HBM and DDR5 – squeezing output of mature-process memory. The [AI-driven memory supercycle](https://www.whalesbook.com/news/English/consumer-products/AI-Sparks-Memory-Supercycle-Driving-Up-Electronics-Prices/69cd17073f30946a723f72cd) is pushing up component costs across consumer electronics and enterprise hardware alike, creating inflationary pressure that extends well beyond the AI sector itself.
## Insurance
The regulators moved in on insurance AI more aggressively than in any other sector this week. A 12-state American pilot, a European regulatory gap analysis and a UK cross-sector supervisory framework all landed within days of each other – while the largest Asian insurer demonstrated what a decade of unencumbered AI investment produces.

heading: [12-state pilot requires carriers to submit AI claims systems for regulatory review](https://www.autobodynews.com/regionalnewsalias/midwest-regional-news/regulators-open-first-examination-of-insurer-ai-behind-total-loss-decisions-and-claims-payouts)
body: A 12-state regulatory pilot launched in March requires American carriers to submit their AI claims systems for direct examination – the first formal regulatory review of insurer AI behind total-loss decisions and claims payouts. The pilot targets the black-box problem: regulators want to understand how AI models assign vehicle values, decide total-loss thresholds and route claims. For carriers, the compliance burden is substantial – documentation of model logic, training data provenance and decision audit trails. Carriers that embedded explainability into their AI architecture from the start will find this manageable; those that deployed opaque models face potential rework costs and enforcement risk.

heading: [Colorado AI insurance regulation deadline approaches on 1 July 2026](https://www.swept.ai/post/colorado-ai-act-insurance-compliance-roadmap)
body: Colorado's SB 21-169 and its implementing Regulation 10-1-1 represent the most prescriptive state-level AI regulation affecting American insurance. Auto insurers and health benefit plan insurers must begin submitting annual compliance reports on 1 July 2026, covering algorithmic fairness testing, impact assessments and governance documentation. The regulation creates a compliance template that other states are likely to adopt, making Colorado a de facto standard-setter for AI governance in American insurance.

heading: [EIOPA flags AI regulatory gap in Insurance Distribution Directive](https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/eiopa-publishes-third-report-application-insurance-distribution-directive-2026-03-30_en)
body: EIOPA's third report on the Insurance Distribution Directive flagged that generative AI is increasingly used through chatbots and sales tools in digital distribution, but the IDD does not comprehensively regulate digital channels or AI-based advice models. The finding is significant because it identifies a gap rather than closing one – a signal that EU-level regulatory action on AI in insurance distribution is being prepared but has not yet arrived. Insurers operating across the EU have a limited window to shape the coming rules through consultation responses.

heading: [FCA publishes 2026 regulatory priorities with AI oversight across all sectors](https://www.tlt.com/insights-and-events/insight/fca-regulatory-priorities-2026-cross-sector-themes-and-what-they-mean-for-firms)
body: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority published its 2026 regulatory priorities across eight sectors – retail banking, mortgages, consumer finance, insurance, consumer investments and others – with AI oversight threaded across all of them. The cross-sector approach means that insurers cannot treat AI governance as an insurance-specific concern; the FCA expects consistent governance standards wherever AI touches consumer outcomes. For UK insurers, this means aligning AI governance frameworks not just with insurance-specific guidance but with the FCA's broader expectations around consumer duty.

heading: [Ping An automates nearly 60% of accident and health claims](https://www.insurancejournal.com/news/international/2026/03/31/864014.htm)
body: Five years ago, almost no accident and health insurance claims at Ping An Insurance Group ran without humans. Today, nearly 60% are automated, with some settled in just 51 seconds – the result of a decade of AI investment worth billions of yuan. The trajectory illustrates a widening gap between insurers that committed early to AI and those still in pilot stages. Ping An's 51-second settlement time on automated claims creates a customer-experience benchmark that competitors in the Asian market – and increasingly in Europe and the Americas – will be measured against.

heading: [Swiss Re flags data centre risk accumulation as unprecedented challenge](https://riskandinsurance.com/data-centers-powering-ai-create-unprecedented-risk-accumulation-challenges-for-insurers/)
body: Construction costs for a single data centre location can exceed $20bn – double once technology is installed – creating concentration exposures in catastrophe-prone areas, according to Swiss Re Institute. The rapid buildout of AI data centre capacity is generating a new class of risk accumulation problem: large single-site losses, correlated failure across co-located facilities and business-interruption exposures that existing actuarial models were not designed to capture. Insurers writing property coverage for hyperscalers and colocation providers face the challenge of pricing a risk class with limited loss history.

heading: [BizCover launches Australia's first business insurance quotes inside ChatGPT](https://australianfintech.com.au/bizcover-launches-australias-first-ai-based-insurance-quotes-within-chatgpt/)
body: BizCover became the first business insurer to offer insurance quoting directly inside ChatGPT, allowing Australian small business owners to receive and compare quotes through a conversational AI interface. The move signals a distribution channel shift: if customers can bind coverage without leaving a chat interface, the traditional broker and comparison-site intermediaries face disintermediation risk. For insurers, the question is whether ChatGPT becomes a distribution platform with bargaining power analogous to that of price-comparison websites.

heading: The compliance clock is ticking, and the vendors know it
body: Multiple podcast hosts this week converged on the observation that AI governance is shifting from a theoretical concern to a procurement requirement. [Vikram Talwar on Insurance Edge](https://insurance-edge.net/2026/04/01/ai-separating-signal-from-noise-in-insurance-operations/) noted that insurance operations teams are spending more time on model documentation than on model development – a sign that the cost of compliance is already baked into deployment economics. The contrast with [Paul Carroll on Insurance Thought Leadership](https://www.insurancethoughtleadership.com/underwriting/genai-takes-underwriting-new-phase) was instructive: Carroll argued that the best underwriting teams are using generative AI to manage portfolios rather than just process individual submissions, and that the carriers who figure out governance first will be the ones who scale AI fastest. The implication across both discussions is that governance is a speed advantage, not a speed tax – a framing that inverts the way most operators think about compliance.

heading: The physical limits that capital cannot buy away
body: Several technical discussions this week grappled with the mismatch between AI funding levels and physical compute constraints. [An analysis on Epoch AI's research blog](https://epoch.ai/data-insights/ai-chip-supply-chain-constraints) detailed how advanced packaging, not chip fabrication, was the binding constraint on AI chip supply in 2025 – a finding that reframes the bottleneck debate away from TSMC's fab capacity and toward the less-discussed assembly and test segment. [Coverage of the memory supercycle on Whalesbook](https://www.whalesbook.com/news/English/consumer-products/AI-Sparks-Memory-Supercycle-Driving-Up-Electronics-Prices/69cd17073f30946a723f72cd) reinforced the point: HBM and DDR5 supply is being rationed in favour of AI customers, pushing up prices for everyone else. The combined picture suggests that the $300bn in Q1 venture funding is chasing a physically constrained supply base, and that the companies with pre-committed capacity agreements hold an advantage that latecomers cannot replicate by writing larger cheques.

heading: When the regulator's AI breaks, everyone's timeline slips
body:
The FDA's forced migration away from Claude inside its clinical review tool attracted pointed commentary. [Analysis on AI Bucket](https://www.aibucket.io/post/fda-scrutinizes-ai-model-migration) explored how a model switch mid-deployment raises validation continuity questions that could affect review timelines for sponsors with active submissions. The episode highlights a dependency most companies have not priced in: their regulator's technology stack is now a variable in their own programme timelines. If the FDA's AI tooling requires revalidation every time a foundation model provider falls out of favour, the knock-on effect is a new source of regulatory delay that has nothing to do with the quality of the sponsor's submission.
Thank you for reading this week's report. Come back next week for all the AI news you need to know in your sector.
